coredata.dc:title: A novel label-free carbon dots-based fluorescent aptamer sensor for the detection of fentanyl
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zheyu
Tong, Yishuo
Liu, Baihui
Dong, Chuan
Yan, Jiangwei
Niu, Weifen
coredata.dc:description: Fentanyl, a widely abused potent synthetic opioid, poses a challenge due to its narrow therapeutic window, increasing the risk of overdose and death. Fast, easy, and cost-effective screening for fentanyl in biological samples is crucial for forensic and public safety purposes. We hereby present a label-free fluorescence sensor for the precise and sensitive identification of fentanyl, utilizing carbon dots (CDs) in conjunction with aptamers. By synthesizing green-emitting CDs in a single step using an environmentally friendly method with o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and thiourea in acetone, the CDs displayed excellent optical properties and biocompatibility. Due to the positive charge of CDs, their fluorescence quenching is caused by electrostatic interactions with negatively charged aptamers. When fentanyl is introduced, the specific binding between fentanyl and the fentanyl aptamer is strengthened, resulting in fluorescence recovery. Under optimal experimental conditions, there was a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and fentanyl concentration in the 5–1000 nM with a limit of determination of 1.90 nM. The robust quantification of fentanyl in complex human saliva matrices achieved by this aptamer-based biosensing platform validates its clinical reliability, particularly for non-invasive point-of-care monitoring and harm-reduction interventions in opioid crisis management.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fentanyl
Aptamer
Carbon dots (CDs)
Fluorescence sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CDs (insert: DLS of CDs); (b) Zeta potential of CDs, aptamer, and before and after binding of CDs and aptamer to fentanyl; (c) FT-IR spectrum of CDs; (d) XPS survey spectrum of CDs.
Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, CDs and fentanyl, CDs and aptamer in the presence and absence of fentanyl.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of fentanyl of varying concentrations: 0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000, 2000 nM in 1 × PBS. (b) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of fentanyl in the range 5–1000 nM in 1 × PBS. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of fentanyl of varying concentrations: 0, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000 nM in 50-fold dilution saliva. (d) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of fentanyl in the range 10–200 nM in 50-fold dilution saliva. The error bars represented the relative standard deviation of three repeated measurements.
Selectivity analysis of the aptamer sensor for fentanyl (200 nM) against other illicit drugs and metabolites (each at 2 µM).
Schematic illustration of fentanyl detection based on fluorescence aptamer sensor.
Comparison results of different methods for fentanyl assay.
Detection of fentanyl in saliva samples.

head.ce:title: A novel label-free carbon dots-based fluorescent aptamer sensor for the detection of fentanyl

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Forensic Medicine, Shanxi Key Laboratory of Forensic Medicine, Shanxi Medical University, Jinzhong 030600 Shanxi, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Key Laboratory of Forensic Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Forensic Medicine, Shanxi Key Laboratory of Forensic Medicine, Shanxi Medical University, Jinzhong 030600, Shanxi, People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006 Shanxi, People's Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, Shanxi, People's Republic of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally: Zheyu Wang and Yishuo Tong.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fentanyl, a widely abused potent synthetic opioid, poses a challenge due to its narrow therapeutic window, increasing the risk of overdose and death. Fast, easy, and cost-effective screening for fentanyl in biological samples is crucial for forensic and public safety purposes. We hereby present a label-free fluorescence sensor for the precise and sensitive identification of fentanyl, utilizing carbon dots (CDs) in conjunction with aptamers. By synthesizing green-emitting CDs in a single step using an environmentally friendly method with o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and thiourea in acetone, the CDs displayed excellent optical properties and biocompatibility. Due to the positive charge of CDs, their fluorescence quenching is caused by electrostatic interactions with negatively charged aptamers. When fentanyl is introduced, the specific binding between fentanyl and the fentanyl aptamer is strengthened, resulting in fluorescence recovery. Under optimal experimental conditions, there was a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and fentanyl concentration in the 5–1000 nM with a limit of determination of 1.90 nM. The robust quantification of fentanyl in complex human saliva matrices achieved by this aptamer-based biosensing platform validates its clinical reliability, particularly for non-invasive point-of-care monitoring and harm-reduction interventions in opioid crisis management.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fentanyl, a commonly abused synthesized opioid receptor agonist, has attracted significant public interest due to its potent effects, rapid onset, addictiveness, and serious societal consequences. Despite its analgesic properties similar to morphine, fentanyl is about 70 times more potent [1]. Its low molecular weight and lipophilic nature contribute to its transdermal permeability, leading to rapid absorption through the skin and increasing the risk of overdose and death if mishandled [2]. As of 2022, fentanyl has emerged as the third most seized drug overall and presently stands as the central contributor to the count of drug-related overdose fatalities in the United States [3,4]. Consequently, many countries have implemented strict regulations to control new psychoactive substances related to fentanyl [5]. Current testing methods for fentanyl analogs, include high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) [6], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [7], electrochemical detection [8], and Raman spectroscopy [9]. While these techniques are precise and sensitive, they heavily rely on advanced laboratory equipment such as mass spectrometers, which are expensive, labor-intensive, and necessitate significant sample quantities and specialized personnel, hindering widespread adoption. For on-site law enforcement detection of fentanyl in body fluids, a sensitive, quick, economical, and easy-to-use detection approach is therefore critical.

Aptamer, an oligonucleotide sequence obtained by SELEX in vitro screening using DNA or RNA libraries, can attach to various targets with high specificity and selectivity and is therefore widely used in the field of biosensors [10,11]. Providing an effective recognition element, aptamers exhibit characteristics such as simplicity of chemical synthesis and modification, cheap cost, high stability, and extended shelf life compared to other recognition elements, making them a perfect option for biosensors [12]. Optical aptamer sensors based on fluorescence emission exhibit various favorable properties including rapid analysis, high sensitivity, and simplicity, and can be classified into labeled and label-free methods [13]. Labeled aptamer sensors need to modify the fluorescent group or quenching group onto the aptamer. The chemical modification is expensive and labor-intensive, and the pairing of the fluorescent group with the quenching group reduces the aptamers’ binding affinity to the target [14]. In contrast, label-free fluorescent aptamer sensors do not have these drawbacks. The selection of fluorescent materials for label-free fluorescent aptamer sensors is an important factor affecting the detection of aptamer signals.

Recently, numerous studies have effectively produced fluorescence sensors employing CDs as probes for detecting ions and small molecules [15–17]. CDs, a new category of zero-dimensional luminescent carbon nanomaterials under 10 nm in size, exhibit a wide source of raw materials, simple synthesis methods, excellent solubility in water, minimal toxicity, outstanding optical characteristics, good biocompatibility, ease of functionalization, and high chemical stability [18]. These characteristics have led to their widespread applications in the fields of fluorescence labeling, biosensing, photocatalytic, drug delivery, and bioimaging [19–21]. Therefore, combining the advantages of CDs and aptamers to construct label-free fluorescent sensors for specific detection of targets will have broad application prospects.

In this study, a positively charged green fluorescent CDs were produced employing a solvothermal method with the precursors OPD along with thiourea in acetone. The aptamer is a nucleic acid sequence with a negative charge. The CDs prepared with positive charge enhanced ssDNA adsorption via electrostatic interactions, leading to significant fluorescence quenching. Upon specific binding to fentanyl at physiologically relevant concentrations, the aptamer undergone a conformational change, which modulated its fluorescence-quenching efficiency. This conformational rearrangement triggered the dissociation of the aptamer-CDs complex, liberating CDs into the solution. The subsequent restoration of CDs fluorescence exhibited a linearity response correlated with fentanyl concentration. Capitalizing on this target-responsive release mechanism, we engineered a label-free biosensing platform that synergistically combines: (i) high-affinity aptamers as molecular recognition switches, (ii) engineered CDs with quantum yield-enhanced fluorescence as signal transducers, (iii) real-time monitoring capability for point-of-care applications.
Materials and apparatus were presented in the Supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the carbon quantum dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were synthesized using a solvothermal method in one single processing step. In this process, 20 mL of acetone was used to dissolve a mixture of OPD (Mw: 108.14, 108 mg) and thiourea (Mw: 76.121, 228 mg). After subjecting the solution to ultrasonic stirring for a duration of 10 min, it was transferred into a 50 mL container made of polytetrafluoroethylene. The mixture then underwent autoclaving at 180 ℃ for 8 h. Once the solvothermal reaction was complete, the autoclave was removed from the oven and allowed to cool to room temperature. The resulting carbon dots were then centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 20 min and stored in a dark location at 4 °C for future use.

The quantum yield of CDs before and after addition of aptamer aptamer was calculated using the method described in the following equation.
Φ x
=
Φ st
D x
/
D st
A st
/
A x
η
x
2
/
η
st
2
Where Φ st represents the standard quantum yield, D stands for the integrated area beneath the emission spectrum, A reflects the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, and η relates to the solvent’s refractive index. The subscript x indicates unknown parameters, while st denotes standard values. We selected quinine sulfate solution (Φ = 0.577 in 0.1 M H2SO4) as the reference standard [22].

Firstly, CDs were diluted 100-fold in 250 μL 1 × PBS (pH 7.4). To this solution, 5 μL fentanyl aptamer (10 μM) was added into the 1.5 ml centrifuge tubes, and set aside at room temperature for 10 min. Next, dilute half of the mixture to a final amount of 5 mL with a pH of 7.4 in a 1 × PBS solution and vortex-mixed thoroughly. Fluorescence signal quantification was ultimately achieved utilizing the microplate reader, which set excitation and emission wavelengths at 316 nm and 502 nm, correspondingly. We designated the fluorescence intensity recorded at this stage as F0 . Subsequently, add fentanyl of different concentrations into the other half, then incubated at 37°C for 20 min. Then diluted the mixture to 5 mL with 1 × PBS solution (pH 7.4) and vortex-mixed thoroughly, then transferred it to 96 well flat bottom black plates for fluorescence detection. The fluorescence intensity measured in this step was denoted as F.

Several drug-free, healthy volunteers had their saliva samples taken, and the samples were spiked with different amounts of fentanyl standard solutions. The samples were then centrifuged and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. Prior to analysis, the collected saliva supernatants were diluted 50 times using a reaction buffer. The samples were assessed to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed sensor, with accuracy validation carried out using HPLC-MS/MS methodology.

The synthesized CDs were subjected to various characterization, including TEM, Zeta potential, FT-IR spectra, and XPS spectral analysis. The morphology of the CDs was examined through TEM imaging. As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, the carbon dots (CDs) exhibited a uniform and nearly spherical shape. The accompanying histogram representing size distribution revealed that the nanoparticle diameters fall between 0.5 and 3.5 nm, with an average size of approximately 2 nm (Fig. 1a inset).

FT-IR and XPS analyses were utilized to characterize the surface functional groups and composition of the CDs. The FT-IR spectrum of the CDs depicted in Fig. 1c, revealed distinct peaks at 1176 cm−1, 1220 cm−1, 1359 cm−1, 1433 cm−1, 1709 cm−1, 2966 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of C–O, C–S, C–N, C=N, C=O, C–H, respectively [23]. Moreover, the broad absorption peak observed between 3000 to 3500 cm−1 was attributed to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations, indicating the surface of CDs is rich in hydroxyl, amine, and amide groups [24]. To elucidate the components of CDs, XPS survey spectra were performed. XPS survey spectra in Fig. 1d, there were five main peaks at 162.3 eV, 226.0 eV, 284.5 eV, 399.1 eV, and 531.7 eV, corresponding to S2p, S2s, C1s, N1s, and O1s respectively, indicating that CDs were mainly composed of C, O, N and S [25,26]. In conclusion, the FT-IR data aligns with XPS results, strongly indicating that the CDs prepared possessed characteristic functional groups such as –COOH, –OH, –NH2, C=O, and C–S, thereby enhancing their remarkable water solubility without the need for any chemical modification [27].

Next, the Zeta potential measurements indicated that the zeta potential of CDs was 34.42 ± 0.69 mV, as shown in Fig. 1b, and this was consistent with the FT-IR analysis results that the CDs surface was rich in amine groups, resulting in a positive charge [28].

To research the optical properties of the prepared CDs, we conducted UV–visible absorption and fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the dominant peak at 299 nm was attributed to π–π* electronic transitions associated with C=C bonds within the carbon core structure. The distinct absorption shoulder at 370 nm corresponded to n–π* transitions facilitated by lone electron pairs introduced through N and S co-doping. Finally, the weaker absorption feature centered at 450 nm likely originated from surface defect states or interfacial charge transfer effects at functionalized carbon dot surfaces, which required further targeted experiments to verify [29–31]. As shown in Fig. S2, we recorded a series of fluorescence emission spectra of CDs excited at various wavelengths (296–336 nm). The experimental results showed that the maximum emission wavelength of the emission spectrum did not change with different excitation wavelengths, and the maximum emission wavelength was all 502 nm. But the fluorescence intensity varied with the excitation wavelength, and when the excitation wavelength was 316 nm, the emission spectrum showed the maximum emission intensity. Therefore, the fixed excitation and emission wavelengths for subsequent experiments were 316 nm and 502 nm. Simultaneously, the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs before and after addition of aptamer was determined to be 42 % and 13 % relative to a quinine sulfate solution, respectively. Furthermore, to verify the fluorescence stability of CDs, variations in the fluorescence intensity of CDs solution were tested within 30 days. The experimental results (Fig. S3) indicated that the fluorescence intensity of CDs remained stable for a period of 30 days, validating its practical applicability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection principle of aptamer sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The detection mechanism of the label-free aptamer fluorescence sensor based on CDs prepared was illustrated in Scheme 1 . The aptamer was a nucleic acid sequence with a negative charge, having a zeta potential value of −3.65 ± 1.06 mV (Fig. 1b). And the CDs prepared with positive charge enabled strong adsorption of ssDNA to the surface of CDs through electrostatic interaction, resulting in the encapsulation of the CDs by the aptamer [32]. Therefore, self-quenching could occur through π–π interactions or resonance energy transfer (RET) mechanisms [33]. Furthermore, the electrostatic interaction between CDs and the aptamer could also cause the aptamer to act as a bridge between the CDs, bringing them closer together, inducing CDs aggregation, and resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity [34]. Meanwhile, the zeta potential value shifted to −52.60 ± 3.64 mV (Fig. 1b), indicating the binding of aptamer and CDs had occurred [35]. In the presence of fentanyl, the specific interaction between the aptamer and fentanyl caused the aptamer to detach from the CDs surface, reducing CDs aggregation. Consequently, the CDs were released, leading to an enhancement in the fluorescence signal.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the system were measured under different experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 2 , CDs emitted strong fluorescent signals. When the aptamer was added into the CDs solution, the fluorescent signal was significantly quenched, which indicated that the electrostatic interaction between the positively charged CDs and the negatively charged aptamer resulted in a decrease in fluorescence intensity. Aptamer got unwrapped from the CDs once fentanyl was added, leading to the restoration of native CDs fluorescence.

This study conducted experiments to determine optimal conditions for CD synthesis. As shown (Fig. S4a and b), fluorescence intensities of CDs varied with reaction time and temperature, confirming their dependence on these parameters. Consequently, 8 h and 180 °C were established as the final synthesis conditions.

To achieve optimal analytical performance for fentanyl detection, we investigated and optimized the experimental conditions for the aptamer sensor, including aptamer concentration, pH, binding temperature and time, as well as incubation temperature and time. Initially, the impact of aptamer concentration on fentanyl detection was studied. By introducing different concentrations (50 nM-2 µM) of aptamer into the reaction system, compared the differences in the relative fluorescence intensity ratio [(F-F 0)/F 0] (F: the recovered fluorescence intensity, F0 : the quenched fluorescence intensity) were compared to determine the optimal aptamer concentration. As shown in Fig. S5, as the aptamer concentration increased from 50 nM to 200 nM, the (F-F0 )/F0 gradually increased accordingly, and as the aptamer concentration increased from 200 nM to 2 µM, (F-F0 )/F0 gradually decreased. Therefore, we chose the optimal aptamer concentration as 200 nM in 1 × PBS solution.

Next, we investigated the influence of pH on the sensing system. 1 × PBS solutions of varying pH (5.0–9.0) were prepared as reaction buffers to compare the difference of (F-F0)/F0 . As illustrated in Fig. S6, there was a steady increase in (F-F0)/F0 as the pH from 5.0 to 7.4, followed by a gradual decline when the pH ranged from 7.4 to 9.0. Consequently, we opted for a 1 × PBS solution at a pH of 7.4 for our reaction solvent.

During the experimental process, it was found that the reaction time and temperature at different reaction stages also had an impact on the detection of fentanyl. Keeping other reaction conditions constant, the binding time and temperature of aptamer and CDs, as well as the incubation after adding fentanyl, were examined. As shown in Figs. S7 and S8, the experimental results indicated that the optimal binding time of aptamer and CDs, and the incubation time of fentanyl with aptamer and CDs were 10 min and 20 min, respectively, with optimal incubation temperatures of 24 °C and 37 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the aptamer sensor
Selectivity and photostability of aptamer sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is a key factor in assessing the analytical detection performance of sensors. The major advantage of aptamers lies in their ability to selectively bind to target molecules. To assess the newly developed aptamer sensor's performance, a comparison study was done using 200 nM fentanyl versus high quantities (2 µM) of various illegal substances and metabolites (Fig. 4 ). The sensor demonstrated a robust fluorescent response to fentanyl, unlike the other substances at tenfold higher concentrations. Such as amphetamine, METH, MDA, MDMA, morphine, 6-MAM, cocaine, codeine, ketamine, methcathinone, and 9-Δ-THC showed only weak fluorescent responses. These findings indicated the excellent selectivity of the aptamer sensor towards fentanyl. Additionally, the stability of the aptamer-based sensor was assessed by monitoring the variations in fluorescence intensity of the bound aptamer and CDs over a duration of 2 h (twice the duration of the entire experimental process). The results, as shown in Fig. S9, indicated that the sensor remained stable throughout the measurement process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the aptamer sensor in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Saliva serves as the most common medium for evidencing drug consumption and evaluating drug exposure, offering a valuable sampling option due to its non-invasive characteristics, straightforward collection process, and high level of acceptance among participants. Saliva samples were obtained from healthy individuals who were not under the influence of drugs. The recovery rates of the assay method were determined by spiking fentanyl at concentrations of 20, 100, and 160 nM into samples, and the outcomes were succinctly presented in Table 2 . The recovery rates of fentanyl obtained from all authentic samples ranged from 99.52 % to 105.33 %. Comparative analysis was conducted using data obtained from the fluorescent aptamer sensor and HPLC-MS/MS, as shown in Table 2, where the results of this method were found to be consistent with those obtained from HPLC-MS/MS, with a relative error margin of less than 10 %, demonstrating the accuracy of the proposed method for fentanyl determination in saliva samples. This indicates that the developed aptamer sensor exhibited satisfactory fentanyl detection performance in authentic samples.

In this work, we engineered a label-free fluorescent biosensor for fentanyl detection by integrating cationic carbon dots as fluorescence transducers and high-affinity fentanyl aptamers as recognition elements. The sensing mechanism leverages electrostatic self-assembly between negatively charged aptamers and cationic CDs, enabling label-free operation through reversible complex formation while maintaining cost-effectiveness via one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of CDs with enhanced photophysical properties and environmental sustainability through biomass-derived precursors. This synergistic design achieved high sensitivity, exceptional specificity, and clinical reliability. With rapid response, portable readout capability, and robust matrix tolerance, the platform demonstrates transformative potential for on-site narcotic screening in forensic investigations and public health harm-reduction programs.

This study was conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki and approved by the Ethics Committee of Shanxi Medical University, Shanxi, China (No: 2020GLL031). All participants provided written informed consent before the start of the experiment.
This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province, China (20210302123303).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126338.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
